blocks.text: Chapter 2.
Anchors and Arbitrary Coherence: The Fallacy of Supply and Demand
The ideas in 30 seconds

Like Konrad Lorenz's goslings imprinting on the first moving object they see, humans imprint on the first price they encounter for a product, and that anchor shapes what they will pay for years afterward.

Ariely, Prelec, and Loewenstein's MIT auction showed that a completely arbitrary number — the last two digits of a Social Security number — swung students' bids by 60 to 120 percent, a phenomenon Ariely calls arbitrary coherence: first prices are arbitrary, but subsequent prices stay logically consistent with them.

Whether an experience is a pleasure worth paying for or a chore requiring compensation is not intrinsic; Ariely's poetry-reading experiment showed a single framing question flips the sign of the price people attach to the same event.

Through self-herding, we treat our own past decisions as evidence of what we value, repeating a first choice (a $4 latte) until it hardens into a habit whose original justification we never re-examine.

Because demand can be manufactured by anchors rather than derived from true preferences, market prices do not reliably reveal the utility of goods — the classical supply-and-demand story is partly a fallacy.
The defense is a deliberate habit audit: trace recurring purchases back to their first decision and ask whether that anchor ever deserved its influence.
Imprinting: Goslings, Black Pearls, and the First Price

Ethologist Konrad Lorenz discovered that goslings attach to the first moving object they encounter after hatching — usually their mother, but in his experiments, Lorenz himself. This imprinting is instant, and it is durable: the goslings followed him through adolescence. Ariely's claim is that human minds treat first prices the same way. The first number we associate with a product becomes an anchor, and future judgments of what the thing is "worth" orbit around it.

The commercial proof is the black pearl. In the 1970s, pearl dealer Salvador Assael partnered with Jean-Claude Brouillet to sell black pearls from Tahitian oysters. His first attempt failed completely — there was no existing market, so buyers had no anchor and no interest. Rather than discount, Assael did the opposite. He persuaded gem dealer Harry Winston to display the pearls in his Fifth Avenue window at extravagant prices, strung alongside diamonds and rubies, and backed the display with full-page ads in glossy magazines.

The strategy worked. By anchoring black pearls to the world's finest gems from their first public appearance, Assael transformed an oddity of dubious value into a luxury that soon hung on the necks of Manhattan's wealthiest women. Nothing about the pearls changed; only the first number attached to them did. That first number then propagated: every subsequent black-pearl price was judged against the luxury anchor, not against the pearls' production cost or any objective quality.

Two refinements matter. First, not every number becomes an anchor — a price only imprints when we actually contemplate buying at it, when we use it as a live reference for a decision. Second, once set, anchors are sticky. They govern not just the anchored product but related ones: an anchor for one flat-screen TV calibrates what seems reasonable for every TV beside it. First decisions echo far longer than we assume.
The Social Security Number Auction: Arbitrary Coherence

To test whether anchors can be truly arbitrary, Ariely, with Drazen Prelec and George Loewenstein, ran an auction with MIT Sloan MBA students. Items included bottles of wine, a cordless keyboard and trackball, a design book, and Belgian chocolates. Before bidding, each student wrote the last two digits of their own Social Security number at the top of the page — and then wrote it again next to each item as a hypothetical price, answering yes or no to "would you pay this much?" Only then did they state their actual maximum bid.

A Social Security number is noise; it carries zero information about the value of wine or keyboards. Yet it dominated the bids. Students whose digits fell in the upper range bid roughly 60 to 120 percent more than students with low digits across the items. Merely considering an arbitrary number as a possible price was enough to imprint it.

Here is the subtle part, and the reason Ariely coined arbitrary coherence: within each student's bid sheet, the prices were perfectly sensible relative to one another. Everyone bid more for the better wine than the cheaper wine, more for the keyboard than the trackball. The relationships among prices were coherent and logical; only the overall level was arbitrary, dragged up or down by a meaningless anchor. This is why anchoring is so hard to notice from the inside — our pricing feels rational because it is internally consistent, even when its foundation is random.

Follow-up experiments with annoying sounds showed the effect persists over time. Participants first quoted a price to endure an unpleasant tone after being anchored at either 10 or 90 cents; their initial anchor kept governing what they demanded across later rounds and different sounds. First decisions do not just influence the moment — they become the baseline for a long chain of subsequent choices.
Tom Sawyer Economics: Is It a Pleasure or a Pain?

Anchoring runs deeper than price levels — it can determine whether an experience counts as good or bad at all. Ariely's inspiration is Tom Sawyer, who reframed whitewashing a fence as a coveted privilege and got other boys to pay him for the chance. Mark Twain drew the moral: "Work consists of whatever a body is obliged to do."

Ariely tested this with poetry. He announced he would give a short recital from Walt Whitman's Leaves of Grass. One group of students was asked whether they would pay $10 to hear him read; another group was asked whether they would accept $10 as payment to sit through it. Then both groups bid on tickets to short, medium, and long readings.

The framing question flipped the market. Students first asked about paying subsequently offered money to attend — and offered more for longer readings. Students first asked about being paid demanded compensation — and demanded more for longer readings. Identical experience, opposite prices, and once again each group was internally coherent: everyone scaled their price sensibly with duration. Neither group had any way to know whether an amateur poetry recital was worth seeking or avoiding, so the first cue they received settled it, and everything after followed logically from an arbitrary starting point.

The unsettling implication is that many of our "preferences" for ambiguous experiences — exotic foods, exercise, networking events, even aspects of our jobs — may rest not on the experience's intrinsic quality but on whichever frame reached us first. Companies exploit this: an experience presented from the start as a premium pleasure gets paid for; the same effort presented as labor must be compensated.
Self-Herding: Standing in Line Behind Yourself

Anchors do not only come from marketers — we generate them ourselves. In ordinary herding, we infer that something is good because other people are doing it (a queue outside a restaurant reads as evidence of quality). Ariely identifies a stranger variant, self-herding: we observe our own past behavior and treat it as evidence of our preferences, lining up behind the earlier version of ourselves.

His example is Starbucks. You once walked in — tired, curious — and paid several dollars for a coffee that Dunkin' Donuts sold for a fraction of the price. The next time you pass, you recall that you did it before, so it must have been worthwhile; you go again. By the third and fourth visits the deliberation is gone entirely. A single questionable decision has compounded into a habit, and the habit into a self-image ("I'm a Starbucks person"), with the original judgment never revisited.

Howard Schultz made that first decision easy to repeat by deliberately breaking the anchor to existing coffee prices. Starbucks was styled as a continental coffeehouse — the aroma of beans, French presses, Italian names like Caffè Americano, Caffè Misto, and Frappuccino, upscale snacks — so that the experience felt categorically different from a doughnut-shop coffee. With no direct comparison available, customers could not use the old Dunkin' Donuts anchor; they were open to a new, much higher one. (This is relativity from Chapter 1 deployed in reverse: instead of inviting comparison, Starbucks engineered its absence.)

Self-herding explains why first decisions deserve outsized care. Each repetition adds another "vote" in the internal record we consult, making the behavior look increasingly justified regardless of whether it ever was. Small initial choices — one latte, one impulse subscription — quietly become long-term policies.
Why Supply and Demand Is Partly a Fallacy — and the Habit Audit

Standard economics says prices emerge where supply meets demand, with demand reflecting consumers' true preferences and the utility they expect. Anchoring breaks this story at the demand side. If willingness to pay can be shifted 60 to 120 percent by a Social Security number, doubled by a shop-window display, or sign-flipped by a single question, then demand is not an independent expression of preferences — it is partly manufactured by the very market forces it supposedly disciplines. Manufacturers' suggested retail prices, advertised "regular" prices, and first-mover pricing all plant the anchors that later masquerade as consumer valuation.

Two consequences follow. First, market prices decouple from true utility: what we pay tracks historical anchors and marketing, not the pleasure or usefulness we actually derive. Second, the relationship between price and demand is more manipulable than textbook curves suggest. Ariely notes, for instance, that consumers' long-run response to price changes depends heavily on remembered anchors — we react fiercely to a price that violates our anchor and gradually re-anchor when the new level persists — which is why he argues policy tools like taxes can reshape behavior once anchors reset. He even suggests the possibility of considering free markets' prices skeptically in domains (like health care or utilities) where anchors, not utility, may dominate.

The personal defense is the habit audit. Because anchors reveal themselves in repetition, interrogate your recurring behaviors rather than your one-off choices. Trace a habit to its first decision and ask: How did this start? Was that first choice reasoned, or was it an anchor someone planted? Does the ongoing pleasure justify the ongoing cost — could the latte money, compounded, buy something you value more? Anchors cannot be deleted, but they can be dethroned: by consciously re-running the first decision with today's information, you can escape a chain of choices that was only ever coherent, never correct.
In practice
Pick one recurring purchase this week (coffee, delivery, a subscription), reconstruct the first time you bought it, and decide fresh whether you would start that habit today at today's price.
Before any significant purchase, write down your own maximum price before looking at the listed price, so the seller's anchor cannot set your baseline.
Treat "regular price," MSRP, and crossed-out prices as planted anchors, not information; compare the offer to substitutes and to what the item is worth to you, not to the tag.
When trying something ambiguous and new — a service, an activity, a job task — notice how it was framed to you (pleasure vs. chore) and deliberately consider the opposite frame before pricing it.
Question one act of self-herding: identify a choice you repeat mainly because you have repeated it, and run a one-week experiment with the cheaper or different alternative.
When setting prices or framing offers yourself, remember that the first number and first frame you present will anchor customers far more than the product's objective features.
